name: Google Glass
description: An optical head-mounted display designed in the shape of a pair of eyeglasses. It failed to gain traction due to privacy concerns, lack of clear use cases for consumers, high price point, and a socially awkward design.

name: Quibi
description: A short-form streaming platform designed specifically for smartphones. It launched during the pandemic when people were at home (not commuting), misjudged the competition from free platforms like TikTok/YouTube, and failed to secure a subscriber base despite massive funding.

name: Juicero
description: A Wi-Fi connected juicer that required proprietary juice packs. It failed when consumers discovered the juice packs could be squeezed by hand faster and just as effectively as the expensive machine, rendering the hardware completely unnecessary.

name: New Coke
description: A reformulation of the classic Coca-Cola recipe intended to combat Pepsi's sweeter taste. It caused massive consumer backlash from loyalists who felt alienated, proving that emotional connection to the original brand was more important than blind taste test results.

name: Amazon Fire Phone
description: Amazon's attempt at a smartphone that featured 3D screen capabilities and a dedicated button to identify objects for purchase on Amazon. It was priced too high to compete with budget phones and lacked the ecosystem to compete with Apple/Samsung, offering features consumers didn't actually want.

name: Kodak Digital
description: Despite inventing the core technology for digital cameras, Kodak management resisted the shift away from film because it was highly profitable. By the time they fully embraced digital, competitors had already captured the market.

name: Blockbuster Total Access
description: Blockbuster's late attempt to compete with Netflix's mail delivery service. It was too little too late, and their reliance on late fees for revenue meant they couldn't pivot their business model fast enough to survive the shift to streaming.

name: Microsoft Zune
description: Microsoft's answer to the iPod. While the hardware was decent, it launched years after the iPod had already established absolute dominance and didn't offer a compelling enough reason for users to switch ecosystems.

name: Theranos
description: Promised revolutionary blood testing from a single drop of blood. It failed spectacularly when it was revealed the technology simply didn't work as claimed, representing a failure of product delivery and massive regulatory/ethical breaches.

name: Segway
description: Hyped as a revolution in urban transport. It failed as a consumer product because it was too expensive, too bulky for sidewalks, illegal on many streets, and solved a problem that walking or biking already solved adequately.

name: Nintendo Virtual Boy
description: An early attempt at 3D VR gaming. It failed due to a monochromatic red display that caused eye strain, high price, lack of true portability, and a very limited library of games.

name: Apple Newton
description: An early personal digital assistant with handwriting recognition. It launched with highly flawed handwriting software that became a cultural joke, and was too expensive and bulky for mass adoption.

name: MoviePass
description: Offered a subscription model allowing users to see a movie a day in theaters for $9.95/month. The unit economics were fundamentally flawed, and the company bled money rapidly as users actually utilized the service.

name: Hoverboards (2015 Craze)
description: Self-balancing scooters that became a massive fad. The market flooded with cheap, unregulated knock-offs containing faulty lithium-ion batteries that caught fire, leading to bans by airlines and retailers.

name: Yik Yak
description: An anonymous, location-based social network popular on college campuses. It failed because it couldn't control severe cyberbullying and harassment on the platform, leading to bans on campuses and a mass exodus of users.
